episode_meta.title: How Germany's Ippen Digital is Fine-Tuning Large Language Models for Their Newsroom

ai_tools: Open-source large language models (pre-trained on German language texts)
DALL-E API (mentioned by host as example)
Parrot (manipulated narrative tracking tool developed during JournalismAI Fellowship)

use_cases.description: Fine-tuning open-source LLMs on millions of regional/local news articles to improve quality for editing, generating, and reversioning content, especially for German-language nuances like subjunctive verb forms in indirect speech
use_cases.outcome: Achieved higher accuracy than proprietary models for task-specific use cases; made fine-tuning accessible even to organizations with fewer resources

use_cases.description: Task-specific LLMs fine-tuned for generating headlines, leads, and summaries
use_cases.outcome: Higher accuracy than proprietary models for specific tasks; editors involved in evaluating accuracy

use_cases.description: Reader-facing chatbots integrated into articles using RAG, allowing readers to ask for additional context on stories using Ippen's own article database as knowledge base
use_cases.outcome: Readers can get reliable, sourced information from the organization's own article archive rather than from an unconstrained LLM

use_cases.description: Follow-up story chatbot allowing readers to ask what happened since an earlier story, pulling from Ippen's article archive
use_cases.outcome: Enables readers to catch up on developing stories using the organization's own reliable content

use_cases.description: Agent-based workflow for fact-checking and misinformation detection, double-checking information during the writing process against pre-selected reliable external sources
use_cases.outcome: Supports editors in fact-checking; validates citations and information before publication

use_cases.description: Tool chaining workflow for sports journalism: AI transcribes a 24-minute video, translates it to German, extracts quotes, generates a summary, produces a first draft, and self-evaluates accuracy against the original transcript
use_cases.tool_used: Chained AI tools (transcription, translation, quote extraction, summarization, draft generation, self-evaluation)
use_cases.outcome: Saves journalists significant time that can be reinvested in original reporting such as opinion pieces or interviews

use_cases.description: Quote accuracy system: two-step approach that first extracts quotes, then instructs the LLM to preserve those quotes unchanged during content generation, combined with highlighting system for editor review
use_cases.tool_used: Custom two-step prompting approach with AI-engineered highlighting system
use_cases.outcome: Reliable quote accuracy at scale across 60 news portals and 250 online editors across nine editorial offices

use_cases.description: Tracking manipulated narratives from Russian state-run media: collected 400,000+ articles from outlets like Russia Today and Sputnik, selected 25,000 about Ukraine, grouped them into 200 semantic topics, and assigned propaganda scores using a pre-trained ML model
use_cases.tool_used: Parrot tool, pre-trained machine learning model for propaganda scoring
use_cases.organization: Ippen Digital, Times of London, SyncPeople (startup)
use_cases.outcome: Created early warning system for journalists to detect coordinated efforts to push manipulated narratives; methodology continued to be refined by SyncPeople across platforms including Truth Social and YouTube

use_cases.description: Modular journalism / personalization: breaking articles into modular pieces to serve different story angles to different readers and different publications within the Ippen network
use_cases.outcome: Political journalists can either choose story angles or receive AI-suggested angles; same journalist-written article can serve different readers with different versions

use_cases.description: AI-powered writing assistant that recommends relevant information from reliable external sources during the writing process using a combination of RAG and agents
use_cases.outcome: Enhances articles with relevant information from verified external sources during the writing process

use_cases.description: Personal Italian language learning tutor built as a multi-prompt process in the ChatGPT app to help his girlfriend learn Italian
use_cases.outcome: Functioning as a personalized language tutor that can ask questions and correct answers

key_claims.claim: LLMs trained primarily on English texts struggle with German subjunctive verb forms needed for indirect speech, leading to wrong attributions where indicative is used instead of subjunctive
key_claims.speaker: Alessandro Alviani

key_claims.claim: Ippen Digital listed more than 200 potential candidate models out of several thousand LLMs before selecting ones to fine-tune
key_claims.speaker: Alessandro Alviani

key_claims.claim: Using LoRA-based efficient fine-tuning enables fine-tuning just a fraction of the parameters while still achieving very good results, making it accessible even to organizations with fewer resources
key_claims.speaker: Alessandro Alviani

key_claims.claim: The fine-tuning process started a couple of months ago and does not take years; at some point during fine-tuning you experience a peak in performance
key_claims.speaker: Alessandro Alviani

key_claims.claim: Having millions of pre-selected and structured articles already available was crucial for speeding up the fine-tuning process
key_claims.speaker: Alessandro Alviani

key_claims.claim: Fully automating AI applications is probably the best way to destroy trust in the media industry
key_claims.speaker: Alessandro Alviani

key_claims.claim: LLMs tend to add a short summary at the very end when reversioning articles for different platforms, which no one wants and doesn't make sense
key_claims.speaker: Alessandro Alviani

key_claims.claim: There is a risk that editors will get used to high quality LLM results and won't be as careful in reviewing outputs as they are today
key_claims.speaker: Alessandro Alviani

key_claims.claim: The industry spent too much time in the last year debating whether AGI has been reached, and far too little time and resources thinking about new AI-assisted workflows
key_claims.speaker: Alessandro Alviani

key_claims.claim: Ippen Digital runs around 60 news portals across Germany with around 250 online editors across nine editorial offices
key_claims.speaker: Alessandro Alviani

key_claims.claim: For the JournalismAI Fellowship project, they collected over 400,000 articles from Russian state-run media, selected 25,000 about Ukraine, grouped them semantically into 200 topics, and used URLs of 8,000 articles to track spread on Twitter
key_claims.speaker: Alessandro Alviani

key_claims.claim: ChatGPT, when released in late 2022, hallucinated a list of US-run biolab sites in Ukraine when prompted, demonstrating the risk of LLM misinformation
key_claims.speaker: Alessandro Alviani

key_claims.claim: German regional media companies struggle with finding enough journalists on a local and regional level
key_claims.speaker: Alessandro Alviani

challenges.description: LLMs trained on English texts produce indicative instead of subjunctive verb forms in German indirect speech, leading to incorrect attributions of quotes
Ensuring quote accuracy at scale across 60 news portals and 250 editors; LLMs tend to alter or misquote when generating or reversioning content
LLMs inexplicably add short summaries at the end of reversioned articles, which is unwanted behavior that must be prevented
Risk of editor complacency: as LLM output quality improves, editors may become less careful in reviewing AI-generated outputs over time
Avoiding full automation of AI applications to maintain trust in the media industry; ensuring human-in-the-loop at all stages
Scaling AI solutions reliably from individual use to 60 news portals across nine editorial offices
Editors should not need to prompt LLMs themselves in daily operations; their strength is reporting, not prompt engineering
Difficulty finding enough journalists at the local and regional level in Germany, making AI assistance tools even more critical
Combating coordinated manipulation of narratives by state-run media on social networks, especially in election periods
RAG-based chatbots can still pull outdated information from similar stories, risking misinformation even with citation sourcing
Requiring that fine-tuned models use open-source models with commercial licenses that are pre-trained on native German texts, not just translated texts

quotes.text: Fully automating applications, AI applications, are probably the best way to destroy trust in the media industry. So that's why it's really important to have this approach is probably just semi-automated or half automated if you like to.
quotes.speaker: Alessandro Alviani

quotes.text: I think we spent really last year we spent a lot of time talking about something that I think is a false problem. And there's basically you already rich artificial general intelligence or not. And I think basically we spend far too little time and resources thinking about new AI assisted workflows.
quotes.speaker: Alessandro Alviani
quotes.topic: Industry focus on AGI debate vs. practical AI workflows

quotes.text: We wanted to have open source, to fine-tune an open source model, not a proprietary model. This model had to offer a commercial license and very important, it has to be basically this model has to be pre-trained on German language texts. Not just on text or articles that have been translated into German.
quotes.speaker: Alessandro Alviani
quotes.topic: Requirements for selecting models to fine-tune

quotes.text: We basically build tools to give them the possibility to improve their reporting and basically free them from repetitive tasks and give them time back, basically. Because as I said, we don't find enough journalists as we would like to.
quotes.speaker: Alessandro Alviani
quotes.topic: Purpose of AI tools in regional journalism

quotes.text: You want to make sure that editors don't need to prompt LLMs themselves. They have other strengths. Their strength is our reporting. That's why we have dedicated prompt editors in the team.
quotes.speaker: Alessandro Alviani
quotes.topic: Division of labor between editors and prompt specialists

quotes.text: The risk is that at some point editors will get used to high quality results coming from LLM and won't be as careful as they are today in reviewing those outputs coming from LLMs.
quotes.speaker: Alessandro Alviani
quotes.topic: Risk of editor complacency with AI outputs

recommendations.advice: Use efficient fine-tuning frameworks like LoRA to keep costs down; this makes fine-tuning accessible even to organizations with fewer resources than larger companies
recommendations.speaker: Alessandro Alviani
recommendations.target_audience: Small and mid-sized media companies considering fine-tuning

recommendations.advice: Always involve editors in the fine-tuning process from the start; developers and editors should work together because editors provide critical feedback on quality and accuracy of results
recommendations.speaker: Alessandro Alviani

recommendations.advice: Build task-specific LLMs rather than trying to use one general model for everything; fine-tune separate models for headlines, leads, summaries, etc.
recommendations.speaker: Alessandro Alviani

recommendations.advice: Have pre-selected and structured data ready before starting fine-tuning; having millions of articles already organized is crucial for speeding up the process
recommendations.speaker: Alessandro Alviani
recommendations.target_audience: Media companies planning to fine-tune LLMs

recommendations.advice: Select open-source models that are natively pre-trained on your language's texts, not models trained on translated texts, to ensure linguistic accuracy
recommendations.speaker: Alessandro Alviani

recommendations.advice: Think about AI in terms of modular tool chaining rather than single-purpose tools; combine transcription, translation, quote extraction, summarization, and self-evaluation into integrated workflows
recommendations.speaker: Alessandro Alviani

recommendations.advice: Never fully automate AI applications; always maintain a semi-automated approach with human-in-the-loop to preserve trust
recommendations.speaker: Alessandro Alviani

recommendations.advice: Build systems that highlight quotes and key information in both original and AI-generated text so editors can easily compare and verify accuracy
recommendations.speaker: Alessandro Alviani

recommendations.advice: Invest in self-evaluating AI systems that can assess the quality of their own outputs before presenting them to editors
recommendations.speaker: Alessandro Alviani

recommendations.advice: Have dedicated prompt editors/engineers rather than expecting journalists to learn prompting; journalists' strength is reporting
recommendations.speaker: Alessandro Alviani
